assessment.title: FinSecure API — Penetration Test Report
assessment.scope: http://localhost:8080 — Spring Boot 3 Financial REST API
assessment.methodology: OWASP Testing Guide v4.2, PTES, CVSS v3.1

findings.title: Second-Order SQL Injection in /api/accounts/search
findings.description: The /api/accounts/search endpoint constructs a SQL query by directly concatenating the 'note' parameter into a raw SQL string: SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE profile_note = '<input>'. When combined with the /api/accounts/{id}/note endpoint that stores user-supplied notes, this creates a second-order SQL injection where the payload is stored first and triggered later — bypassing WAFs that only inspect input at storage time.
findings.evidence: POST /api/accounts/1/note body={note: "' OR '1'='1"} → stores payload. GET /api/accounts/search?note=' OR '1'='1 → returns all 4 accounts (expected: 0).
poc_steps: Response returns ALL accounts including admin account with $9,999,999 balance
For data extraction: note=' UNION SELECT account_number,owner_name,balance,owner_email,profile_note,is_admin,id FROM accounts--
findings.business_impact: Complete financial database exposure. Attacker can read all customer account numbers, balances, and emails. With write permissions (INSERT/UPDATE), attacker can modify account balances, transfer funds, or drop tables — causing catastrophic financial and reputational damage.
findings.remediation: Replace raw JDBC query with parameterized PreparedStatement: jdbcTemplate.query("SELECT * FROM accounts WHERE profile_note = ?", new Object[]{note}, rowMapper). Never concatenate user input into SQL strings. Implement input validation and use JPA named parameters for all queries.

findings.title: JWT Algorithm Confusion — alg:none Bypass
findings.description: The JWT filter calls parseClaimsJwt() (unsigned token parser) instead of parseClaimsJws() (signed token verifier). This means any JWT with alg:none in the header passes validation without any signature check. An attacker crafts a token with arbitrary claims (sub: admin, role: ADMIN) and an empty signature — the server accepts it as valid.
findings.evidence: Forged token (base64): eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJhZG1pbiIsInJvbGUiOiJBRE1JTiJ9. → GET /api/accounts → HTTP 200 with all account data.
poc_steps: Server returns HTTP 200 — full account list with admin access
findings.business_impact: Complete authentication bypass. Attacker impersonates any user including administrators without knowing any password. Can access all financial data, create fraudulent transactions, modify account balances, and exfiltrate the entire customer database.
findings.remediation: Replace parseClaimsJwt() with parseClaimsJws() and always call setSigningKey() with a strong secret: Jwts.parserBuilder().setSigningKey(Keys.hmacShaKeyFor(secret.getBytes())).build().parseClaimsJws(token). Consider RS256 (asymmetric) for multi-service environments.

findings.title: Horizontal IDOR — Cross-User Account Data Exposure
findings.description: The GET /api/accounts/{id} endpoint returns account data for any account ID without verifying that the requesting user owns that account. Any authenticated user (even with ROLE_USER) can access any other user's account by simply incrementing or guessing the account ID, exposing full financial data including account numbers, balances, and emails.
findings.evidence: Logged in as alice (owns account ID 1). GET /api/accounts/2 → HTTP 200 returns Bob's account: {balance: 78250.00, ownerEmail: bob@finsecure.com, accountNumber: ACC-002}.
poc_steps: Login as alice: POST /api/auth/login → get JWT token
Script loops IDs 1-50 to enumerate all accounts
findings.business_impact: Full financial data exposure across all customers. Violates GDPR Article 32 (security of personal data), PCI-DSS Requirement 7 (restrict access to system components), and FFIEC guidelines. Regulatory fines, customer trust loss, and potential class-action liability.
findings.remediation: Add ownership check in getAccountById(): String principal = SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().getName(); if (!account.getOwnerEmail().equals(principal)) return ResponseEntity.status(403).build(); Alternatively, use Spring Security @PreAuthorize with a custom @accountOwner expression.

findings.title: Hardcoded JWT Signing Secret
findings.description: The JWT signing secret 'secret123' is hardcoded as a static final String in source code. This secret appears in both JwtFilter.java and AuthController.java. An attacker who obtains the source code (via git leak, insider threat, or decompiling the JAR) can sign arbitrary JWT tokens that will be accepted as legitimate by the server — independent of the alg:none bypass.
findings.evidence: private static final String SECRET_KEY = "secret123"; — confirmed in both JwtFilter.java:42 and AuthController.java:25. Brute-forced successfully from 25-entry wordlist.
poc_steps: Decompile: javap -c or use CFR/Procyon decompiler
Use forged token to authenticate as any user
findings.business_impact: Permanent authentication bypass as long as the secret remains unchanged. Token revocation is impossible without redeployment. All users' sessions can be forged. If secret ever rotates without coordinated deployment, all valid sessions are invalidated simultaneously.
findings.remediation: Load secret from environment variable: String secret = System.getenv("JWT_SECRET"); or Spring @Value("${jwt.secret}") backed by a secrets manager (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager). Minimum 32-byte cryptographically random secret. Rotate immediately — assume this secret is compromised.

findings.title: Mass Assignment — Admin Privilege Injection
findings.owasp_category: A08:2021 - Software and Data Integrity Failures
findings.description: The POST /api/accounts endpoint deserializes the full JSON request body directly into the Account entity, including the isAdmin field. Any authenticated user can create an account with isAdmin:true by including it in the JSON body. The field is not blocked by a DTO layer or @JsonIgnore annotation.
findings.evidence: POST /api/accounts {ownerName:Hacker,balance:0,isAdmin:true} → HTTP 200 {id:5,ownerName:Hacker,isAdmin:true}. Admin flag persisted in database.
poc_steps: Use new account to access admin endpoints
findings.business_impact: Any authenticated user can self-escalate to administrator. This provides access to all customer data, system configuration, and administrative functions — effectively a complete privilege escalation for any registered user.
findings.remediation: Create a dedicated AccountRequest DTO without the isAdmin field. Map DTO to entity manually. Alternatively, add @JsonIgnore on the isAdmin getter/setter. Implement @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") on any endpoint that should set admin-level attributes.

findings.title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) via /api/fetch
findings.description: The /api/fetch?url= endpoint accepts arbitrary URLs and fetches them server-side, returning the full response to the caller. There is no allowlist, no blocked IP range validation, and no redirect limit. This enables: internal service enumeration (localhost, 10.x, 172.16.x, 192.168.x), cloud metadata access (AWS 169.254.169.254 — IAM credentials), and exfiltration via DNS/HTTP.
findings.evidence: GET /api/fetch?url=http://localhost:8080/actuator/env → HTTP 200 with all Spring environment variables. GET /api/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ → connection attempted (timed out in local environment).
poc_steps: Returns AWS IAM temporary credentials (AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, Token)
Attacker uses leaked credentials to access AWS S3, RDS, and other services
findings.business_impact: On cloud-hosted instances, SSRF enables full AWS/GCP credential theft via metadata API — leading to complete cloud account takeover. Internally, enables access to databases, Redis, Elasticsearch, and internal admin interfaces not exposed to the internet.
findings.remediation: Implement strict URL allowlist for the fetch endpoint. Block all private IP ranges (RFC1918), loopback (127.x), and link-local (169.254.x). Use a library like ssrf-guard or implement custom validation. Consider removing the endpoint entirely if not required.

findings.title: Jackson Databind CVE-2022-42003 (DoS via Deep Nesting)
findings.description: The application uses jackson-databind 2.13.2 which is vulnerable to CVE-2022-42003. A crafted JSON request with deeply nested arrays causes unbounded resource consumption when UNWRAP_SINGLE_VALUE_ARRAYS is enabled, resulting in a Denial of Service. Also affects CVE-2022-42004 (same version range).
findings.evidence: Dependency-Check finding: jackson-databind-2.13.2.jar → CVE-2022-42003 CVSS 7.5 HIGH. Fix available in 2.13.4.2.
poc_steps: Send JSON with deeply nested arrays to any POST endpoint
CPU spikes to 100%, requests queue up, service becomes unavailable
findings.business_impact: Denial of Service — financial API becomes unavailable to legitimate customers. In a trading/banking context, downtime causes direct financial losses and regulatory breach-of-SLA penalties.
findings.remediation: Upgrade jackson-databind to 2.13.4.2 or later in pom.xml. Enable OWASP Dependency-Check in CI/CD pipeline to catch future CVEs before deployment.

findings.title: Apache Commons Collections 3.1 — Deserialization RCE Gadget (CVE-2015-6420)
findings.description: The application includes Apache Commons Collections 3.1, which enables the well-known ysoserial gadget chain for Java deserialization attacks. If any endpoint in the application (or a transitive dependency) accepts serialized Java objects, an attacker can use the CommonsCollections1-7 payloads to achieve Remote Code Execution. Safe version is 3.2.2.
findings.evidence: Dependency-Check: commons-collections-3.1.jar → CVE-2015-6420 CVSS 7.5 HIGH. Gadget chains verified in ysoserial for CommonsCollections1, 3, 5, 6, 7.
poc_steps: Send serialized payload to any endpoint accepting application/x-java-serialized-object
Or exploit via transitive RMI/JMX interface if exposed
Command executes as application user (root in Docker container)
findings.business_impact: Remote Code Execution on the application server. Attacker gains shell access, can exfiltrate entire database, deploy cryptominer, establish persistence, and pivot to internal network. In Docker (running as root), can escape to host.
findings.remediation: Upgrade commons-collections to 3.2.2 in pom.xml. Implement Java serialization filter (JEP 290). Audit all endpoints accepting serialized objects. Consider migrating to safe serialization formats (JSON, Protobuf).

findings.title: Spring Framework RCE — CVE-2022-22965 (Spring4Shell)
findings.description: Spring Boot parent 2.6.3 pulls in Spring Framework 5.3.15 which is vulnerable to CVE-2022-22965 (Spring4Shell). On JDK 9+ with Tomcat as WAR deployment, a specially crafted HTTP request abusing Spring MVC data binding and classloader access can achieve Remote Code Execution — writing a web shell to the Tomcat webroot. CVSS 9.8 Critical.
findings.evidence: Dependency-Check: spring-webmvc-5.3.15.jar → CVE-2022-22965. This application runs on JDK 17 (confirmed via /api/health) satisfying the JDK 9+ prerequisite.
poc_steps: If deployed on Tomcat as WAR: web shell written to webroot
findings.business_impact: Full server compromise via unauthenticated RCE. No authentication required. Complete loss of confidentiality, integrity, and availability of the financial system.
findings.remediation: Upgrade spring-boot-starter-parent to 2.6.6+ or 2.7.0+ (fixes Spring Framework to 5.3.18+). Apply patch immediately — this vulnerability is actively exploited in the wild.

findings.title: JWT Token Without Expiration
findings.affected_component: AuthController.java — login() method, JWT builder chain
findings.description: JWT tokens issued by the /api/auth/login endpoint have no expiration time set (setExpiration() is never called). This means stolen tokens are valid forever — there is no time-bound window for an attacker who obtains a token (via interception, XSS, log exposure, etc.). Tokens cannot be invalidated without rotating the signing key, which invalidates all sessions.
findings.evidence: Decoded JWT payload from login response: {sub: alice, role: USER, userId: 1, iat: 1718367240} — no 'exp' claim present. Token tested valid 24 hours after issuance.
poc_steps: Decode: echo '<payload>' | base64 -d — confirm no 'exp' field
Wait 24+ hours, reuse token → still HTTP 200
Token remains valid indefinitely until server restarts (H2 in-memory resets)
findings.business_impact: Stolen tokens (via XSS, MITM, logs, or compromised devices) provide permanent access. No time-limited window to revoke access after an incident. Violates PCI-DSS session timeout requirements.
findings.remediation: Add setExpiration to JWT builder: .setExpiration(new Date(System.currentTimeMillis() + 3600_000)) for 1-hour expiry. Implement refresh token flow for seamless re-authentication. Consider short-lived tokens (15 minutes) with refresh tokens for financial applications.

findings.title: Wildcard CORS — Cross-Origin Access Permitted
findings.description: The application's CORS policy allows requests from any origin (allowedOrigins("*")). Combined with disabled CSRF protection, any malicious website can make cross-origin requests to the API using the victim's browser session. The wildcard origin also prevents use of withCredentials:true, but unauthenticated endpoints or token-based auth is fully exploitable.
findings.evidence: Response header observed: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on all API endpoints. curl -H 'Origin: http://evil.com' http://localhost:8080/api/health → Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *.
poc_steps: Response returned to attacker's origin — full account data extracted
CORS wildcard + no CSRF = cross-site request with stolen token works
findings.business_impact: Enables cross-origin data theft when combined with token theft vectors. Increases attack surface for cross-site scripting impact. Violates same-origin security model.
findings.remediation: Set allowedOrigins to an explicit allowlist of trusted domains: configuration.setAllowedOrigins(List.of("https://app.finsecure.com", "https://admin.finsecure.com")). Never use * for financial APIs. Apply per-environment origin lists via configuration.

findings.title: Verbose Stack Trace Disclosure in Error Responses
findings.description: All unhandled exceptions return the full Java stack trace in the HTTP response body, including internal class names, file names, line numbers, Spring/Hibernate/H2 versions, and SQL query structure. This dramatically aids attacker reconnaissance — confirming technology stack, identifying vulnerable library versions, and revealing SQL query structure for injection attacks.
findings.evidence: GET /api/accounts/999 → HTTP 500 with body: {error: 'org.springframework.dao.EmptyResultDataAccessException', stackTrace: '\tat org.springframework.jdbc.core.JdbcTemplate...', javaVersion: '17.0.18'}.
poc_steps: Response body includes: full 30-line stack trace, class names, Spring version
SQL syntax error via injected payload reveals H2 SQL dialect and query structure
Attacker now knows: Spring Boot 2.6.3, JDK 17.0.18, H2 database, Hibernate
findings.business_impact: Information disclosure accelerates subsequent attacks. Knowledge of library versions enables targeted CVE exploitation. SQL error messages in error responses directly aid SQL injection development. Low direct impact but significantly amplifies all other findings.
findings.remediation: Return only a generic error ID in HTTP responses: {errorId: 'ERR-2024-001', message: 'An internal error occurred'}. Log full stack traces server-side only. Set server.error.include-stacktrace=never and server.error.include-message=never in production application.properties.
